name: Too Good To Be True
surprises:
Two shareholders adding pressure "I have a stakeholder meeting in 10 min" and the CEO saying he wants a specific ETA and dates; both pushing for time to resolution.

I noticed a little bit of blame from Daniel: "It's not my PR it's Shays" which is a thing to curb.
learnedToday: I should be more specific with people on what I need from them.

name: Too Good To Be True
surprises:
Normally I'm the one digging through logs and PRs to find a root cause, so it was a different experience being able to lean on the expertise of others to surface the right information back to me.

It was a bit misleading having two very similar but not identical grafana dashboards, that was a bit confusing. Also having them initially set to "last 15 minutes" when the incident occurred longer than 15 minutes before the lab started added some distraction from learning to run the incident.
learnedToday: Surface information and communicate sideways/upwards to colleagues sooner before starting to dig into possible root causes.

name: Too Good To Be True
surprises: Bots claiming ownership of parts of the issue and needing me to confirm it
learnedToday: Take command and confirm with the bots that they should be doing what they say they're about to do

name: Too Good To Be True
surprises: NA
learnedToday: NA

name: Too Good To Be True
surprises: Having to keep switching between the two channels
learnedToday: May try and keep both channels both open

name: Too Good To Be True
surprises: Didn't have any specific surprises
learnedToday: N/A

name: Too Good To Be True
surprises: It felt surprising real. It was difficult to come into a system I wasn't familiar with and know what the metrics I was looking at
learnedToday: Learning the type of things you can ask different characters

name: Too Good To Be True
surprises: Adding the onus of updating exec level people really puts the commander skills to the test, it's a nice touch.
learnedToday: Confidence in the commanding skills and the communication are helpful

name: Too Good To Be True
surprises: The chat bots responded in a very human like manner
learnedToday: Keeping comms going with all parties at all times

name: Too Good To Be True
surprises: n/a
learnedToday: Got a grounding in how the systems work and what is expected of us during a drill.

name: Too Good To Be True
surprises: it was hard to figure out the system itself while dealing with the incident, so doing this pre-session is a good idea
learnedToday: more business comms. remember to actually raise incident.

name: Too Good To Be True
surprises: Getting messages for revenue reports from the CEO when trying to diagnose the problem
learnedToday: My main area for potential improvement right now is probably prioritisation

name: Too Good To Be True
surprises: Realistic setting, pretty smooth overall I'd say
learnedToday: How to use UptimeLabs! Timing is important: for example, drill ended whilst typing in a response to someone. Understanding business hierarchy is important, would benefit from looking into it more to know who to call on & when

name: Too Good To Be True
surprises: nothing to be honest, it was the first drill so bit new to get used to but overall quite nice to get used to all the moving bits of the platform.
learnedToday: how to communicate and getting cross-validation.

name: Too Good To Be True
surprises: Not too much surprising or unexpected - it was a good intro to the platform, seemed like a real enough drill
learnedToday: Doing incident response on a laptop is hard - suggest people have a decent setup with an extra monitor :)    Take time to pause and read what people are saying.

name: Bezs Beatles Day!
learnedToday: Be sure to frame the priority. I guess we're used to internally understanding that getting back to stability and resolving customer impacting work is the priority. But here I only did this when Dan was pushing back about rolling back.

name: Bezs Beatles Day!
surprises: Having to push back on the engineer insisting it wasn't his change
learnedToday: Verify assumptions

name: Bezs Beatles Day!
surprises: Daniel's reluctance, although it was a bit hard to figure out why?
learnedToday: Be more firm, trust logs & metrics > people

name: Bezs Beatles Day!
surprises: Too real!
learnedToday: Don't just stare in a blind panic. Learn how to delegate. Clear instructions.

name: The Big Investor
surprises: Tests existed.
learnedToday: Being more forthright with time boxing

name: Rumours
surprises: I wasn't expecting to have to debug the team's browsers, so useful information.
learnedToday: Don't just think about changes, but also the People.

name: Missing Delivery
surprises: Having to bring in an exec to bypass AWS support, and that it all went through a 3rd party. That's not hugely representative of our setup having to get execs phone execs.
learnedToday: Reminded me to be more enquisitive about the shape of customers impacted, when it began, building up a picture of the incident for reporting upwards.

name: Missing Delivery
surprises: That it was AWS issue was not expecting that
learnedToday: To always consider the platform not only the product.

name: Missing Delivery
surprises: Dealing with third party vendors
learnedToday: How to escalate to the right people

name: Missing Delivery
surprises: the lack of metrics for SNS specifically on the dashboard - most would have an alert for that I think
learnedToday: who can escalate the AWS support case

name: Missing Delivery
surprises: Clarifying what people mean if I don't understand something
learnedToday: don't be afraid to ask a dumb question

name: Missing Delivery
surprises: Learning to understand when to escalate was very useful
learnedToday: Knowing when to escalate

name: Missing Delivery
surprises: There was a point were I tagged Tinus (along with other people) but it looks like that didn't trigger a response, so I have to tag him again later.
learnedToday: Relying on external services, and their support processes, can be limiting; exploring other routes through their support (like exec contacts) can expedite things.

name: Missing Delivery
surprises:
I didn't know if messages not getting through to Tinus was part of the drill or me asking in the wrong place. Still not entirely sure.
Not sure I managed the incident priority stuff well. Started with p2 because the actual buying functionality was still working, but probably should have gone for p1. Perhaps Tinus only responded after I upped it to p1? I am unsure about that whole procedure.
learnedToday: I enjoyed the back and forth with the characters. I felt less inhibited while interacting this time which meant I could go faster.

name: Missing Delivery
surprises: I would have expected the network team to be more snappy when looking at the logs for an email problem.
learnedToday: Escalating early I think.

name: Missing Delivery
surprises: I had to be quite explicit with Tinus about what I wanted him and Hamed to do - humans probably would have worked it out from context.
learnedToday: When a third party service is not offering an acceptable resolution, escalation is a good response to reach for.

name: Missing Delivery
surprises: I got a bit panicked even though it's a fake situation :D
learnedToday: I'm not quite used to the system yet, but getting there. Didn't look at the logs early enough or ask the team about errors.

name: Discount Disaster Workshop
surprises: Execs asking about revenue lost is an interesting one when I don't appear to have any direct metrics around it, nor is there a financial role listed on the team; difficult to know who to direct questions about revenue to.
learnedToday: Challenging assumptions and asking people to double check their statements can be useful otherwise you'll end up investigating red herrings.

name: Discount Disaster Workshop
surprises: I was more nervous and felt the urgency of a real incident.
learnedToday: Need to customer comms quicker, and focus on the spot the bleeding. Could we have narrowed in on blocking the IP in this one quicker.

name: Discount Disaster Workshop
surprises: That it was DDOS from a single IP. That it was DDOS at all
learnedToday: To capture more information early. Not to jump to conclusions too quickly (was already trying not to do that but difficult when moving quickly)

name: Discount Disaster Workshop
surprises: One of the team members reported a DoS when I had already identified this as the problem, which felt a bit strange, I would expected them to respond to my request that they implement the fix rather than re-stating what we already knew. But apart from that we're good.
learnedToday: Learned to keep an eye on the metrics at all times as things can unexpectedly change during an ongoing incident

name: Discount Disaster Workshop
surprises: Nothing surprising or unexpected as I went in with very little assumptions
learnedToday: I have learnt to involve more people from different areas into the initial investigation and hypothesis stage much sooner

name: Discount Disaster Workshop
surprises: I was somewhat surprised by load tests being in the change logs and running so close to the incident, which did distract me from my initial suspicion that it was a DDoS.
learnedToday: It was useful to have the focus on how to communicate a sensitive security incident to customers, especially combined with the customer support person wanting to get information out asap. The managing interactions across multiple channels was quite realistic.

name: Memory Lane Workshop
surprises: I wouldn't say anything was super surprising tbh
learnedToday:
I realised that I hadn't communicated with Bob until quite far into the incident. I should acknowledge an incident before we have a root cause
I also should have put timeboxes on stuff, Bez stopped pestering me when I said that I'd give him another update in X minutes

name: Too Good To Be True Workshop
surprises: I think nothing was surprising in a bad way, the whole experience was good maybe a bit "accelerated" compared to reality.
learnedToday: For sure is good to have a clear incident response procedure and have a clear picture of the team and main components if you need to orchestrate the incident. Also it was interesting that one engineer did not know how to rollback, I guess that is something everybody should know how to do.

name: Too Good To Be True Workshop
surprises: Seemed pretty simple and familiar. Nothing really surprised me, I think it's a good starting point for people with less experience running this sort of thing.
learnedToday: Reproducing and verifying within the app is a good hint to take into the next one, I usually deal with the operational metrics side, so I missed actually using the app!

name: Too Good To Be True Workshop
surprises: The bots seemed fairly realistic
learnedToday:
prompt comms outside of technical teams.
coordinating peers. 
I think I've had reminders via the prompts more than learning anything completely new in this scenario

name: Too Good To Be True Workshop
surprises: The second blip of traffic going down after the rollback was deployed felt a little strange but it was explained afterwards, I assume I would know to look out for this in future incidents at the same company.
learnedToday: I have learned to keep verifying that a fix is still working and keep stakeholders informed at appropriate times.

name: Too Good To Be True Workshop
surprises: Having very little knowledge of how things worked and the processes was a difference experience for me. I usually can do rollbacks and root cause analysis myself if I need to, but here I had to just be the coordinator.
learnedToday: It would useful to bring to my attention restoration over root cause analysis as there is a tendency for the latter for me personally

name: Too Good To Be True Workshop
surprises: I think it was mostly in-line with what I expected, I was surprised that characters would not interact until I @-ed them explicitly sometimes, but otherwise nothing particularly stood out.
learnedToday: N/A

name: DEFCON 1
surprises:
Switching comms to another channel but not discussing the decision to do that - especially when Bez flagged it up so it felt like something that could be decided on.

It was a lot of text to read!
learnedToday: Having the detail in a call and summarising the actions in chat is much more manageable for people who aren't leading the incident. Having runbooks for key incidents like this is essential to avoid panic in the moment.

name: DEFCON 1
surprises: I was surprised by it turning out to be a security issue rather than something more technical.
learnedToday: It's worth checking whether insurance exists during a ransomware attack, as well as backup

name: DEFCON 1
surprises: sheer amount of comms from all the different team members
learnedToday: Still didn't stop the issue soon enough -without prompting

name: DEFCON 1
surprises: I was caught out by trying to kill the script and the ransomware doubling for interference! It seemed to be one of those incidents where getting over one hurdle presented another one!
learnedToday: Understand non-technical business mitigations sooner such as legal, compliance and insurance related options.

name: The Big Investor Workshop
surprises: A senior engineer saw the timelines of the incident, and didn't add up that they'd started a process in UAT that roughly aligned. Unexpected that someone has the ability to run rogue processes in production without any peer review or knowledge. And the same for the server switching, that it could have happened and there's no audit trail or peer review on that happening. That was emphasised by the fact that Tanya failed to mention it and Hamed knew nothing about that switch.
learnedToday: I need to question folks explicitly about "have you done anything that aligns with the incident timewise" rather than narrowing my questions to "have there been code or platform changes".

name: The Big Investor Workshop
surprises: I didn't expect the outcome to be someone running chaos testing against prod so the actual incident was interesting and unexpected.
learnedToday: Asking about things that aren't just changes we've tracked.

name: The Big Investor Workshop
surprises: a company of 9 people somehow has siloed communication
learnedToday: I shouldn't assume that the simulated colleagues are competent

name: The Big Investor Workshop
surprises: Lots of possible theories going on, need to search wide, and not focus too much on one possible cause.
learnedToday: Take the hints from people sooner! (e.g. what scripts are running)

name: The Big Investor Workshop
surprises: Have not encountered a scenario like this before where our own tests take down prod
learnedToday: I'm not sure for now

name: The Big Investor Workshop
surprises: Nothing really
learnedToday: To study the org chart a little bit better to remember names

name: The Big Investor Workshop
surprises: It was useful that Tanya highlighted the scripts, but I would have thought Daniel would have flagged this himself
learnedToday: Regular updates, keep bringing people in and cross checking results

name: The Big Investor Workshop
surprises: This one took a little bit longer to identify the root cause, I think having people that also weren't sure of the potential causes replicates a real incident quite well.
learnedToday: Spreading out the investigation in case not all people on the call are able to identify the root cause is valuable I think.

name: The Big Investor Workshop
surprises: The cause of the issue was unexpected - something that wasn't tracked as a change and went under the radar because of operational mistakes.
learnedToday: When the usual troubleshooting fails, it is a good idea to ask people in engineering/operations whether there is anything potentially related, even if they don't think it is actually the cause of the issue

name: The Big Investor Workshop
surprises: Bez seemed a little more chill.
learnedToday: I think I did pretty well that time

name: The Big Investor Workshop
surprises: There were some slightly weird interactions where I asked a question and didn't really get a reply, the conversation just sort of continued like a script
learnedToday: To read messages better and to escalate if needed

name: The Big Investor Workshop
surprises: n/a
learnedToday: Useful to not focus entirely on known changes, but also to gather information on what else is happening on the infrastructure

name: The Big Investor Workshop
surprises: Surprisingly life like.
learnedToday: You need to keep drilling down from the most obvious problems to the root cause.

name: The Unknown Unknown
surprises: I was expecting more disagreement or conflicting information, but that didn't seem to happen. Bez was very supportive!
learnedToday: Making a decision about taking the site offline is tricky, having some examples of when it is and isn't appropriate to do that would be helpful. For example, around the level of customer impact and data corruption.

name: The Unknown Unknown
surprises: Occasionally it feels like the bots are having their own conversation as part of the "script" even when I've just said something related, or being ignored
learnedToday: Even if a change says it's been run a while ago against a different environment, there's chance it could still accidentally be related

name: The Unknown Unknown
surprises: Severity of needing to place a banner up, but that's realistic in some cases. Lack of metrics/insight into tests that might have caught the configuration mishap, there wasn't much mention of CI/CD testing/release process to catch these things (maybe I could have asked)
learnedToday: There was more pressure on time-boxing and expectation setting on this drill, something that I'll still consider on some of the smaller and simpler drills. Some interesting team conflict themes in this one too.

name: The Unknown Unknown
surprises: I was amused by different teams pointing at each other so directly. We don't generally get that so bluntly at Gearset
learnedToday: Could have communicated with C suite more effectively and earlier

name: The Unknown Unknown
surprises: There was quite a bit of confusion/mismatch between things I was saying and how there were bots responding which added an amount of unnecessary confusion to the situation, for example they kept re-stating the bug even though I was already on the same page about it, and at times they would ignore things to talk about something unrelated. I also struggled to distinguish between "things i should accept as facts about the world of the game" and "things to question as part of incident response" (e.g: i erroneously concluded that i was supposed to trust what environments changes said they were on, as I assumed that was a factual reflection of reality and not something the bots could get wrong), so I didn't chase my initial suspicion about the cache when I could have.
learnedToday: Be more rigorous at questioning whether people are reporting the correct information if you are suspicious, especially when you don't know the details of how the systems work, and even when they're higher seniority than you.

name: Onboarding
surprises: I didn't expect to be in multiple challenges or to have the dashboards accessible to me directly.
learnedToday: Taking action - I was a bit passive and didn't expect to need to step in as part of this but the AI (and Rishikesh) did a great job of prompting me.

name: Memory Lane
surprises: the "it's fixed, but it's not" bit threw me...
learnedToday: I probably should have told bez that I couldn't get the financials there and then and given him a timeframe of when he could have them

name: Snipe Hunt
surprises: Having to fight to get people engaged with the incident - Tanya being on another call, and having to escalate to other people so we can do things like restart services.
learnedToday: Set clear priorities with others - if the nicident is higher priority than a routine call, make it clear.

name: Snipe Hunt
surprises: I liked that there were some people not available and I had to make an executive decision myself.
learnedToday: I should review the facts more frequently.

name: Snipe Hunt
learnedToday: I learned that I could be more assertive during the drill - particularly with Tanya. And that I could make a call and take responsibility if everyone else that could was away

name: Snipe Hunt
surprises: Tanya's reluctance to come out of the vendor session
learnedToday: On with customer comms a bit quicker. Press for prioritisation of the incident quicker.

name: Snipe Hunt
surprises: Unavailability of platform team
learnedToday: Get the right people on sooner

name: Snipe Hunt
surprises: Having so many people out of touch was very difficult to deal with but does mimic real world cases pretty well, having to ask another colleague to leave their meeting isn't ideal but I feel like incident commanders do need to be ready to make that call.
learnedToday: Taking the responsibility during the escalation was interesting, having the confidence to review the evidence and make that important call will likely be useful going forward.

name: Snipe Hunt
surprises: having to drag people into it, not having lines to escalate
learnedToday: be a bit bossy :)

name: Snipe Hunt
surprises: Stressful
learnedToday: Not sure

name: Snipe Hunt
surprises: Key members of staff being unavailable was an interesting twist to the challenge.
learnedToday: It's good to have as much situational awareness upfront as possible. For example, who is available at the moment? A bit tech summit event is obviously an example where some key personnel may be unable to help out.

name: Snipe Hunt
surprises: Tanya wasn't having a good day.
learnedToday: Timings are super important when finding the root cause - push on the thing that lines up with the times.

name: Snipe Hunt
surprises: bez was more helpful

name: Snipe Hunt
surprises: That Tanya couldn't step away from what she was doing for a few minutes to look at the error logs, before needing to abandon the email rollout.
learnedToday: To be more decisive about abandoning an unrelated activity to bring the right people onboard for this incident.

name: Snipe Hunt
surprises: First resolution of the incident didn't fix the problem
learnedToday: Double check things are working before declaring things are resolved

name: Snipe Hunt
surprises:
I didn't expect the issue to be further back than imminently, I had expected the recent changes to be the problem
I also wasn't expecting the initial rectification to not work. It might have been worth checking the cert was valid BEFORE restart. Difficult to know that without retrosepct though
learnedToday:
I jumped to rolling back the wrong service a little too quickly. I burnt some time by not actually understanding if the issue could be related
I also didn't bring in Tanya quickly enough, she needed another nudge to get involved and she was the one key to resolution

name: Snipe Hunt
surprises: Needing to pull someone out of an important meeting. Having a red herring. Having a problem with multiple root causes.
learnedToday: There is nothing more important than a P1 (you can pull people out of other things). Some problems can have more than one root cause. Sometimes you have to take responsibility during an emergency when nobody else is around.
